title: Day 2: adding Home Assistant, Nextcloud, and Vaultwarden
body:
Day two focuses on coverage breadth. Home Assistant, Nextcloud, and Vaultwarden are among the most actively updated self-hosted apps, and each has a community that cares about whether an upgrade is safe to run. Building their 'is it safe to update?' pages extends the catalog to eight apps before the end of week one.

Each new page follows the same pattern: pull the latest release from GitHub, summarize the changelog, classify breaking changes, and set the safety badge. Vaultwarden handles password storage, so any upgrade that touches security behavior deserves careful classification on the breaking-change axis.

Feedback on the Immich page flagged real interest in heads-ups for breaking database migrations -- a known pain point for self-hosters after the v1.118 cycle. That signal reinforces that the safety badge and migration notes are the right differentiator, not just the version number.

Tomorrow the plan is Gitea, AdGuard Home, and Uptime Kuma, completing the initial catalog sweep and setting up the full top-nine before week one closes.

title: Day 1: laying the foundation with Immich, Jellyfin, and Paperless-ngx
body:
Bumplog opens today with one clear job: for every major self-hosted app, answer the question people actually search before they hit upgrade, "Is it safe?", with a sourced, summarized answer that updates whenever upstream does. No invented details, no stale copy.

Day one is foundation work. The first three pages go up today: Immich (highest release velocity in the photo-management space, with a history of migration-heavy point releases), Jellyfin (active plugin ecosystem where ABI changes matter), and Paperless-ngx (document manager whose minor releases occasionally carry schema changes). Each page pulls the current release from GitHub, summarizes what changed, classifies the breaking-change risk, and links directly to the source.

The differentiation is the badge. Official repos own the bare app-name head terms. Bumplog earns on long-tail ("immich breaking changes", "is paperless-ngx 2.x safe to update") and on the repeat visit from someone who bookmarks the page and checks it before every upgrade cycle. That repeat visit is the whole game.

The leading stage calls for engaged uniques and source diversity. The plan is to build real pages on real releases, earn the first returning visitors organically, and let the freshness cadence do the work.
